linked_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. We construct attacks using this side-channel in two common LLM tasks: recovering the target language in machine translation tasks and recovering the output class in classification tasks. In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM.
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes a side-channel attack to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses and using this to fingerprint query categories. summary: Your research idea shows very low originality, with significant overlap in problem formulation, methodology, and domain with existing work, particularly "Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count." To improve originality, consider a novel application domain or a fundamentally different approach to timing side-channel analysis.

papers.title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
papers.abstract: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. We construct attacks using this side-channel in two common LLM tasks: recovering the target language in machine translation tasks and recovering the output class in classification tasks. In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM. Our experiments show that an adversary can learn the output language in translation tasks with more than 75% precision across three different models (Tower, M2M100, MBart50). Using this side-channel, we also show the input class in text classification tasks can be leaked out with more than 70% precision from open-source LLMs like Llama-3.1, Llama-3.2, Gemma2, and production models like GPT-4o. Finally, we propose tokenizer-, system-, and prompt-based mitigations against the output token count side-channel.

The attacker seeks to leverage the output token count timing side-channel and the length of the entire encrypted output and input, to leak private attributes with high accuracy." | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on side-channel attacks on LLM inference APIs. The attacker seeks to leverage the output token count timing side-channel... to leak private attributes with high accuracy." | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is a 'new timing side-channel in LLMs due to variations in output token counts' that 'enables attackers to infer private information using execution time measurements,' which directly matches the user's idea of 'recovers approximate input token counts by timing the streaming token-generation responses, then uses this to fingerprint the query category.' papers.title: InputSnatch: Stealing Input in LLM Services via Timing Side-Channel Attacks
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) possess extensive knowledge and question-answering capabilities, having been widely deployed in privacy-sensitive domains like finance and medical consultation. During LLM inferences, cache-sharing methods are commonly employed to enhance efficiency by reusing cached states or responses for the same or similar inference requests. However, we identify that these cache mechanisms pose a risk of private input leakage, as the caching can result in observable variations in response times, making them a strong candidate for a timing-based attack hint. In this study, we propose a novel timing-based side-channel attack to execute input theft in LLMs inference. The cache-based attack faces the challenge of constructing candidate inputs in a large search space to hit and steal cached user queries. To address these challenges, we propose two primary components. The input constructor employs machine learning techniques and LLM-based approaches for vocabulary correlation learning while implementing optimized search mechanisms for generalized input construction. The time analyzer implements statistical time fitting with outlier elimination to identify cache hit patterns, continuously providing feedback to refine the constructor's search strategy. We conduct experiments across two cache mechanisms and the results demonstrate that our approach consistently attains high attack success rates in various applications. Our work highlights the security vulnerabilities associated with performance optimizations, underscoring the necessity of prioritizing privacy and security alongside enhancements in LLM inference.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'InputSnatch,' proposes a timing-based side-channel attack to 'execute input theft in LLMs inference' by exploiting cache mechanisms to 'construct input to match inputs from normal users' and determine 'cache hit patterns' (Abstract, Threat Model). While both use timing side-channels on LLM inference, the paper's goal is input theft and reconstruction, whereas the user's idea is about recovering token counts for query fingerprinting, representing a different specific problem within the same broad topic. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose timing-based side-channel attacks on LLM inference APIs. The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming token generation responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'InputSnatch,' also uses timing side-channels, specifically 'prefill time' differences due to cache hits/misses in prefix and semantic caching, to 'steal input' by reconstructing user queries. While both use timing, the paper's method involves a more complex 'input constructor' and 'time analyzer' to reconstruct specific input fields or semantic content, rather than just approximate token counts for fingerprinting. | Domain: The user's idea focuses on fingerprinting query categories without seeing the input, implying a broader application. The paper, while also a timing side-channel attack on LLMs, specifically targets 'input theft' to recover 'partial or complete input' in 'medical consultation system' and 'online legal consultation services', which are specific application targets within the broader domain of LLM security. | Contribution: The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming token-generation responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'InputSnatch,' proposes a timing-based side-channel attack to 'execute input theft in LLMs inference' by exploiting cache-sharing mechanisms (prefix and semantic caching) to reconstruct candidate inputs, using a 'time analyzer' to identify cache hit patterns. While both use timing side-channels on LLM inference, the user's goal is token count recovery for query fingerprinting, whereas the paper's goal is input theft/reconstruction based on cache hits, which are distinct contributions.

papers.title: The Early Bird Catches the Leak: Unveiling Timing Side Channels in LLM Serving Systems
papers.abstract: The wide deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) has given rise to strong demands for optimizing their inference performance. Today's techniques serving this purpose primarily focus on reducing latency and improving throughput through algorithmic and hardware enhancements, while largely overlooking their privacy side effects, particularly in a multi-user environment. In our research, for the first time, we discovered a set of new timing side channels in LLM systems, arising from shared caches and GPU memory allocations, which can be exploited to infer both confidential system prompts and those issued by other users. These vulnerabilities echo security challenges observed in traditional computing systems, highlighting an urgent need to address potential information leakage in LLM serving infrastructures. In this paper, we report novel attack strategies designed to exploit such timing side channels inherent in LLM deployments, specifically targeting the Key-Value (KV) cache and semantic cache widely used to enhance LLM inference performance. Our approach leverages timing measurements and classification models to detect cache hits, allowing an adversary to infer private prompts with high accuracy. We also propose a token-by-token search algorithm to efficiently recover shared prompt prefixes in the caches, showing the feasibility of stealing system prompts and those produced by peer users. Our experimental studies on black-box testing of popular online LLM services demonstrate that such privacy risks are completely realistic, with significant consequences. Our findings underscore the need for robust mitigation to protect LLM systems against such emerging threats.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. While both involve timing side channels and LLMs, the specific problem of recovering 'approximate input token counts' for 'fingerprinting query categories' is not directly addressed as the main problem in the paper; instead, the paper focuses on recovering the content or semantics of prompts. | Method: The user's idea focuses on timing streaming token generation to recover approximate input token counts for fingerprinting query categories. The paper, while also using timing side channels in LLM serving systems, primarily exploits shared KV caches and semantic caches to infer confidential system prompts or other users' prompts, and to detect cache hits/misses. Both approaches leverage timing, but the specific mechanisms and immediate goals (input token count vs. prompt content/category) differ meaningfully. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on side-channel attacks on LLM inference APIs using timing measurements. The user's idea specifically targets recovering approximate input token counts to fingerprint query categories, while the paper explores timing side channels from shared caches (KV cache and semantic cache) to infer confidential system prompts or user prompts. Both are concerned with privacy implications in LLM serving systems and exploit timing discrepancies in token generation or response times. | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is the discovery and exploitation of timing side channels in LLM serving systems, specifically targeting KV cache and semantic cache, to infer confidential prompts. This directly aligns with the user's research idea of a side-channel attack on LLM inference APIs that recovers approximate input token counts by timing streaming token-generation responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper explicitly discusses using 'timing measurements' and 'classification models' to 'detect cache hits' and 'infer private prompts with high accuracy', and details 'token-by-token search algorithm to efficiently recover shared prompt prefixes', which is a direct match to the user's proposed method and goal.

papers.title: Spill The Beans: Exploiting CPU Cache Side-Channels to Leak Tokens from Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Side-channel attacks on shared hardware resources increasingly threaten confidentiality, especially with the rise of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this work, we introduce Spill The Beans, a novel application of cache side-channels to leak tokens generated by an LLM. By co-locating an attack process on the same hardware as the victim model, we flush and reload embedding vectors from the embedding layer, where each token corresponds to a unique embedding vector. When accessed during token generation, it results in a cache hit detectable by our attack on shared lower-level caches. A significant challenge is the massive size of LLMs, which, by nature of their compute intensive operation, quickly evicts embedding vectors from the cache. We address this by balancing the number of tokens monitored against the amount of information leaked. Monitoring more tokens increases potential vocabulary leakage but raises the chance of missing cache hits due to eviction; monitoring fewer tokens improves detection reliability but limits vocabulary coverage. Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate the feasibility of leaking tokens from LLMs via cache side-channels. Our findings reveal a new vulnerability in LLM deployments, highlighting that even sophisticated models are susceptible to traditional side-channel attacks. We discuss the implications for privacy and security in LLM-serving infrastructures and suggest considerations for mitigating such threats. For proof of concept we consider two concrete attack scenarios: Our experiments show that an attacker can recover as much as 80%-90% of a high entropy API key with single shot monitoring. As for English text we can reach a 40% recovery rate with a single shot. We should note that the rate highly depends on the monitored token set and these rates can be improved by targeting more specialized output domains.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'Spill The Beans,' addresses the problem of leaking *output* tokens from LLMs using CPU cache side-channels to recover exact token sequences (e.g., API keys or English text). While both involve side-channel attacks on LLMs and token leakage, the specific mechanism (timing streaming responses vs. cache monitoring) and the target (input token count for fingerprinting vs. output token content for direct leakage) represent different angles on the problem. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a side-channel attack based on timing streaming token-generation responses to infer approximate input token counts and fingerprint query categories. In contrast, the paper 'Spill The Beans' introduces a novel cache side-channel attack that leaks *output* tokens by monitoring cache hits on embedding vectors in shared CPU/GPU memory. The methods are fundamentally different: one relies on network-level timing of output stream length, while the other relies on hardware-level cache monitoring of embedding layer accesses. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on side-channel attacks against LLM inference APIs. The user's idea targets recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories, while the paper (Spill The Beans) uses cache side-channels to leak generated output tokens (e.g., API keys, English text) from LLMs. Both are in the domain of LLM security and side-channel attacks, but the specific target (input vs. output tokens) and mechanism (network timing vs. CPU cache) differ slightly. | Contribution: The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'Spill The Beans', introduces a novel cache side-channel attack to leak *output* tokens from LLMs by monitoring embedding layer accesses, and uses this to recover sensitive data like API keys or plain English text. While both involve timing side-channels on LLMs, the target (input vs. output tokens), the specific side-channel (streaming response timing vs. CPU cache monitoring), and the immediate goal (input token count for query fingerprinting vs. exact token recovery) are distinct.

papers.title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
papers.abstract: As post-training techniques evolve, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly augmented with structured multi-step reasoning abilities, often optimized through reinforcement learning. These reasoning-enhanced models outperform standard LLMs on complex tasks and now underpin many commercial LLM APIs. However, to protect proprietary behavior and reduce verbosity, providers typically conceal the reasoning traces while returning only the final answer. This opacity introduces a critical transparency gap: users are billed for invisible reasoning tokens, which often account for the majority of the cost, yet have no means to verify their authenticity. This opens the door to token count inflation, where providers may overreport token usage or inject synthetic, low-effort tokens to inflate charges. To address this issue, we propose CoIn, a verification framework that audits both the quantity and semantic validity of hidden tokens. CoIn constructs a verifiable hash tree from token embedding fingerprints to check token counts, and uses embedding-based relevance matching to detect fabricated reasoning content. Experiments demonstrate that CoIn, when deployed as a trusted third-party auditor, can effectively detect token count inflation with a success rate reaching up to 94.7%, showing the strong ability to restore billing transparency in opaque LLM services. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/CASE-Lab-UMD/LLM-Auditing-CoIn.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on a side-channel attack to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. The paper, 'CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs', addresses the problem of 'token count inflation' where providers overreport or inject fabricated reasoning tokens for billing purposes. While both deal with token counts in LLM APIs, the paper's problem is about auditing billing transparency and detecting fraudulent token reporting, rather than a side-channel attack for input token count estimation and query fingerprinting. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a side-channel attack using timing of streaming token-generation responses to infer input token counts and fingerprint query categories. The evaluated paper, CoIn, proposes a verification framework that audits hidden reasoning tokens using verifiable hash trees from token embedding fingerprints and semantic validity checks with matching heads. These are fundamentally different methodologies; one is a timing-based side-channel attack, and the other is a cryptographic and semantic auditing framework. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the domain of Large Language Model (LLM) APIs and side-channel-like analysis. However, their specific application targets differ. The user's idea focuses on recovering approximate input token counts for query category fingerprinting, while the paper, 'CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs,' aims to audit hidden reasoning token counts for billing transparency and detect token count inflation. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a side-channel attack to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query categories. In contrast, the paper 'CoIn' proposes a verification framework to audit the quantity and semantic validity of *hidden reasoning tokens* in commercial LLM APIs to detect token count inflation for billing transparency, using hash trees and embedding-based relevance matching. The methodologies and objectives are distinct: one is an attack to infer input characteristics, the other is an auditing framework to verify hidden output tokens for billing integrity.

existing_examples.name: Side-channel attacks on LLMs (general)
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers explore side-channel attacks on LLMs, focusing on various aspects like model extraction, data leakage, and prompt inference. While not all specifically target 'approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses,' the general concept of using timing or resource consumption as a side channel for LLMs is a known research area.

existing_examples.name: Timing attacks (general)
existing_examples.description: Timing attacks are a well-established category of side-channel attacks in cryptography and computer security, where an attacker analyzes the time taken to perform cryptographic operations to gain information about secret data. Applying this to LLM inference is a natural extension of existing timing attack principles.

existing_examples.name: LLM fingerprinting/categorization without direct input access
existing_examples.description: Research exists on inferring properties of LLM inputs or the nature of queries (e.g., whether it's a coding query, a factual question, etc.) without direct access to the full input, often relying on output characteristics or metadata. Using timing to achieve this specific goal aligns with this broader research direction.

existing_examples.name: Research on LLM API security and vulnerabilities
existing_examples.description: There's a growing body of research dedicated to identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities in LLM APIs, including those related to performance characteristics, resource usage, and the information that can be gleaned from observing API interactions.

reality_check.assessment: The core idea of using timing information from LLM API responses as a side channel to infer properties about the input (specifically, approximate token counts for query categorization) is not entirely novel. Side-channel attacks, including timing attacks, are a well-established field in computer security. Applying these principles to LLM APIs, and specifically using response timing to infer input characteristics, is a known area of research. While the exact combination of 'approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query category' might not be described verbatim in every paper, the underlying components and methodologies are present in existing research on LLM security, side-channel attacks, and inference of input properties.
novelty_aspects: Specific methodology for precisely recovering 'approximate input token counts' from streaming responses (e.g., specific timing points, aggregation methods).
Novel techniques for mapping these approximate token counts to 'fingerprint query categories' with high accuracy and robustness.
Demonstration of this attack's effectiveness against specific, widely-used LLM inference APIs and its practical implications.
reality_check.recommendation: While the general concept exists, there's still room for novel contributions in the specific implementation, accuracy, and practical impact. Focus on developing a highly effective and robust methodology for recovering token counts and mapping them to categories. Clearly articulate how your approach differs from and improves upon existing side-channel attacks on LLMs. Consider demonstrating the attack's feasibility against specific, popular LLM APIs and discussing its implications for API design and security.

reality_check_warning:
⚠️ **Warning: This idea may already exist!**

Your idea closely resembles **Side-channel attacks on LLMs (general)** (similarity: 80%).

*The core idea of using timing information from LLM API responses as a side channel to infer properties about the input (specifically, approximate token counts for query categorization) is not entirely novel. Side-channel attacks, including timing attacks, are a well-established field in computer security. Applying these principles to LLM APIs, and specifically using response timing to infer input characteristics, is a known area of research. While the exact combination of 'approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses to fingerprint query category' might not be described verbatim in every paper, the underlying components and methodologies are present in existing research on LLM security, side-channel attacks, and inference of input properties.*

**Recommendation:** While the general concept exists, there's still room for novel contributions in the specific implementation, accuracy, and practical impact. Focus on developing a highly effective and robust methodology for recovering token counts and mapping them to categories. Clearly articulate how your approach differs from and improves upon existing side-channel attacks on LLMs. Consider demonstrating the attack's feasibility against specific, popular LLM APIs and discussing its implications for API design and security.

matched_sections.text_snippet: Attack Scenario. matched_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. We construct attacks using this side-channel in two common LLM tasks: recovering the target language in machine translation tasks and recovering the output class in classification tasks. In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes a side-channel attack to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses and using this to fingerprint query categories. The paper explicitly demonstrates this exact side-channel, its mechanism (timing via autoregressive generation), and its application to recover sensitive information (like input class/query category) in LLMs.

matched_sections.paper_title: InputSnatch: Stealing Input in LLM Services via Timing Side-Channel Attacks
matched_sections.heading: Cache Optimization in LLM Inference
matched_sections.text_snippet: LLMs generate text autoregressively, producing one token at a time conditioned on the initial prompt and the preceding generated tokens. The generation process typically involves two phases: prefill and decoding . The prefill phase computes input prompts intermediate states (keys and values) for input prompts and generates the first next token. The decoding phase generates output tokens autoregressively one by one until a stopping token is met.

matched_sections.paper_title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: However, to protect proprietary behavior and reduce verbosity, providers typically conceal the reasoning traces while returning only the final answer. This opacity introduces a critical transparency gap: users are billed for invisible reasoning tokens, which often account for the majority of the cost, yet have no means to verify their authenticity. This opens the door to token count inflation, where providers may overreport token usage or inject synthetic, low-effort tokens to inflate charges.

Both deal with token counts in opaque LLM APIs, but the user's problem is about input tokens and fingerprinting query categories, whereas the paper's problem is about auditing hidden reasoning tokens for billing transparency.

matched_sections.paper_title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
matched_sections.heading: Preliminary
matched_sections.text_snippet: Billing is based on the total number of tokens m + n , including the invisible reasoning tokens. As Figure 1 shows, reasoning tokens often dominate the total count, i.e., m ≫ n , resulting in a significant transparency gap. Token Count Inflation. We consider two strategies for inflating token counts: - Naive token count inflation. COLA reports a falsified token count m f > m , leading to direct overbilling without modifying the output. - Adaptive token count inflation. Anticipating user-side defenses (e.g., hash matching, spotchecking), COLA may append low-effort fabricated reasoning tokens to the original reasoning trace.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea is about recovering input token counts, while the paper's problem is about verifying invisible reasoning tokens (output-side) for billing and detecting inflation. matched_sections.paper_title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: With this shift, providers like OpenAI increasingly adopt new service models. Reasoning traces, while critical for quality, are often verbose, sometimes speculative Jin et al. [2024], Zhang et al. [2025], and may reveal internal behaviors vulnerable to distillation Gou et al. [2021], Sreenivas et al. [2024]. To protect proprietary methods and streamline outputs, commercial APIs typically suppress these intermediate steps, exposing only the final answer. However, users are still charged for all generated tokens, including those hidden from view. We refer to such services as Commercial Opaque LLM APIs (COLA) -proprietary, pay-per-token APIs that conceal both intermediate text and logits.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are directly concerned with 'LLM inference APIs' and the opaque nature of commercial LLM services.

matched_sections.paper_title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: To address this issue, we propose CoIn, a verification framework that audits both the quantity and semantic validity of hidden tokens. CoIn constructs a verifiable hash tree from token embedding fingerprints to check token counts, and uses embedding-based relevance matching to detect fabricated reasoning content. Experiments demonstrate that CoIn, when deployed as a trusted third-party auditor, can effectively detect token count inflation with a success rate reaching up to 94.7%, showing the strong ability to restore billing transparency in opaque LLM services.
matched_sections.reason: The user's contribution is to recover approximate input token counts and use them to fingerprint query categories. The paper's contribution is a framework (CoIn) to audit the quantity and semantic validity of hidden reasoning tokens to detect billing inflation. While both deal with token counts in LLMs, the specific contributions (input token recovery for fingerprinting vs. hidden token auditing for billing transparency) are distinct.

matched_sections.paper_title: CoIn: Counting the Invisible Reasoning Tokens in Commercial Opaque LLM APIs
matched_sections.heading: Conclusion
matched_sections.text_snippet: This paper presents CoIn , a novel auditing framework designed to verify the token counts and semantic validity of hidden reasoning traces in COLA. We identify and formalize the problem of token count inflation , in which service providers can overcharge users by injecting redundant or fabricated reasoning tokens that are not visible to the user. To address this, CoIn integrates two complementary components: a hash tree-based token quantity verifier and a semantic relevance-based validity checker. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that CoIn can detect both naive and adaptive inflation strategies with high accuracy, even under limited exposure settings. By enabling transparent and auditable billing without revealing proprietary content, CoIn introduces a practical mechanism for accountability in commercial LLM services.
matched_sections.reason: The user's contribution is focused on recovering input token counts and using them for query category fingerprinting. The paper's contribution is an auditing framework for hidden reasoning tokens to ensure billing integrity. The goals and resulting contributions are different.

sentence_annotations.sentence: A side-channel attack on LLM inference APIs that recovers approximate input token counts by timing the streaming token-generation responses, then uses this to fingerprint the query category without seeing the actual input.

linked_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. ... In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM.
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea directly states the problem of a side-channel attack on LLM inference APIs to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses. The paper's abstract describes demonstrating a new side-channel to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in LLMs based on output token count, and that this can be recovered reliably using a timing channel.

linked_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM. ... Using this side-channel, we also show the input class in text classification tasks can be leaked out with more than 70% precision from open-source LLMs like Llama-3.1, Llama-3.2, Gemma2, and production models like GPT-4o.

The core mechanism of timing to infer token counts and then using that for fingerprinting is very similar.

linked_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. ... We construct attacks using this side-channel in two common LLM tasks: recovering the target language in machine translation tasks and recovering the output class in classification tasks.

linked_sections.paper_title: Time Will Tell: Timing Side Channels via Output Token Count in Large Language Models
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: This paper demonstrates a new side-channel that enables an adversary to extract sensitive information about inference inputs in large language models (LLMs) based on the number of output tokens in the LLM response. We construct attacks using this side-channel in two common LLM tasks: recovering the target language in machine translation tasks and recovering the output class in classification tasks. In addition, due to the auto-regressive generation mechanism in LLMs, an adversary can recover the output token count reliably using a timing channel, even over the network against a popular closed-source commercial LLM.
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes a side-channel attack to recover approximate input token counts by timing streaming responses and using this to fingerprint query categories. The paper explicitly demonstrates this exact side-channel, its mechanism (timing via autoregressive generation), and its application to recover sensitive information (like input class/query category) in LLMs.
